title: Power
summary: How fast energy moves. Wield P = W/Δt and P = F·v on cars, climbs, and hard accelerations — and see why power grows with v while energy grows with v².

steps.title: How fast you move energy
steps.body:
Two movers carry the same stack of boxes up the same stairs. One takes 1 minute, the other takes 10. They did the **same work** — but the fast one was far more **powerful**.

**Power** is the *rate* at which energy is transferred. Same job + less time = more power.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
**W** is the work done (J), **Δt** is the time it took (s). Power is measured in **watts (W)**, where 1 watt = 1 joule per second.

Set the work and the time below — watch power shoot up as you shrink the time.

steps.prompt: You do a fixed **W = 120 J** of work. Drag the **time** you take and watch your power output.
steps.task: Drag the time down to **half** — from 4 s to 2 s. Then read the two bars.
steps.solution: Power is work over time: P = W / Δt. The work stays 120 J, so cutting the time from 4 s to 2 s halves the denominator — which doubles the power, from 30 W to **60 W**.

steps.title: Power and speed
steps.body:
There's a second face of power. If a force F pushes something moving at speed v, the work piles up as F·d while the distance grows as d = v·Δt. Divide and the time cancels:

P = W/Δt = F·d/Δt = F·v.

So for steady motion against a steady force, power is simply **force times speed**.

steps.prompt: A car pushes against a steady **F = 600 N** of drag. Drag the car's **speed** and watch the power the engine must deliver.
steps.task: **Double** the speed — drag it from 10 m/s to 20 m/s. Then compare the two power bars.
steps.solution: With a fixed force, P = F·v is **linear** in speed: doubling v from 10 to 20 m/s exactly doubles the power, 6000 W → 12,000 W. Worth pausing on — the *kinetic energy* of that car grows with v² (quadruples), but the *power* to push it against a steady force grows only with v. Energy and power scale with speed differently.

steps.title: Watch a P = F·v problem worked
steps.prompt: A car's engine drives it forward with a steady **600 N** while it cruises at a constant **25 m/s**. How much power is the engine delivering?
steps: Steady force, steady speed — use the speed form of power directly: P = F·v.
steps.takeaway: P = F·v is the shortcut whenever something moves at constant speed against a constant force — no work or time needed.

steps.prompt: Your turn — setup's done. A motor does **3000 J** of work in **6.0 s**. What's its power output?
shownSteps: Power is work over time: P = W / Δt

steps.prompt: A car cruises at a **constant 30 m/s** down the highway. Air resistance and friction together push back with **900 N**. What power must the engine deliver to hold that speed?
hints: Constant speed means the engine force just balances the 900 N of drag. Use P = F_A·v.
steps.solution:
At constant speed the engine's drive force equals the 900 N of resistance, so use P = F_A·v.

P = 900 × 30 = **27,000 W** (27 kW, about 36 horsepower).

steps.prompt: A **50 kg** hiker climbs a steady slope that rises **1 m for every 10 m** traveled along it (so sin θ = 0.10), moving at a constant **4.0 m/s** up the slope. Ignoring friction, what power does she generate against gravity? (g = 9.8)
hints: On an incline, the force she fights is the component of gravity along the slope: F = mg·sin θ.
steps.solution:
Climbing at constant speed, the hiker must match the slope component of gravity: F = mg·sin θ = 50 × 9.8 × 0.10 = 49 N.

Then power is force times speed along the slope: P = F·v = 49 × 4.0 = **196 W**.

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **65 kg** athlete sprints up a staircase that rises **4.5 m** vertically, taking **5.0 s** at a steady pace. (g = 9.8)

**Part A —** How much work does the athlete do against gravity?
hints: At a steady pace there's no change in KE, so all the work raises gravitational PE: W = mgh.
steps.solution:
Running at a steady pace, the work goes into lifting the body: W = mgh.

W = 65 × 9.8 × 4.5 ≈ **2867 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** What is the athlete's power output on the climb?
hints: Power is the work done divided by the time it took.
steps.solution:
Power is work over time: P = W / Δt.

P = 2866.5 / 5.0 ≈ **573 W** — well above a sustainable human output, which is why a hard stair sprint is exhausting.

steps.prompt:
**Challenge — link motion to power.** A **1000 kg** car accelerates from rest to **20 m/s** on level ground in **8.0 s**. Ignore friction and drag.

**Part A —** How much work does the engine do over those 8.0 s? (Hint: work-energy theorem.)
hints: From rest, the net work equals the kinetic energy gained: W = ½mv².
steps.solution: By the work-energy theorem, the work done equals the kinetic energy gained from rest: W = ½mv² = ½ × 1000 × 20² = ½ × 1000 × 400 = **200,000 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** Over that same **8.0 s**, what was the engine's *average* power output?
hints: Average power is the total work divided by the total time.
steps.solution:
Average power is total work over total time: P_avg = W / Δt = 200,000 / 8.0 = **25,000 W** (25 kW).

This ties the whole unit together — kinetic energy from the work-energy theorem, then power as the rate that energy was delivered.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body:
You connected work, time, force, and speed: used P = W/Δt and P = F·v, climbed an incline against gravity, measured a real athlete's stair-sprint power, and linked a hard acceleration to the engine power behind it.

That's the full Work, Power & Energy toolkit — now go mix it all together.
